questions.stem: According to the lecture, what does Dijkstra's principle state about testing?

options.text: Testing can demonstrate the presence of bugs, but not their absence
Testing can demonstrate the absence of bugs, but not their presence
Testing proves a program is correct once every test passes
Testing is only meaningful when 100% coverage is reached

questions.explanation: Dijkstra's maxim, quoted verbatim on the slides, is 'Testing can demonstrate the presence of bugs, but not their absence.' The inverted 'absence not presence' wording is wrong, and the claims that passing tests prove correctness or that only full coverage gives meaning both overclaim, since a green suite is evidence not proof. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: What is a Turing machine, as defined in the lecture?

options.text: A physical computer Alan Turing built to break the Enigma cipher
A simple mathematical model of computation that manipulates symbols on a tape based on rules, yet can implement any computer algorithm
A test harness that automatically generates inputs for any program
A compiler that guarantees a program will halt

questions.explanation: The deck defines a Turing machine as a simple mathematical model of computation manipulating symbols on a tape by rules, able to implement any algorithm. The distractors describe a physical machine, a fuzzer, and a halting oracle. (Software Testing p.3)

questions.stem: Which problem is the theoretical root cause of testing incompleteness?

options.text: The halting problem, inherited by Turing-complete languages

questions.explanation: Turing-complete languages inherit Turing's halting problem, which makes a perfect test suite theoretically impossible. P vs NP is about complexity, Byzantine generals about consensus, dining philosophers about deadlock. (Software Testing p.4-5)

questions.stem: The lecture says a perfect test suite is theoretically impossible. What is the best a programmer can do?

options.text: Prove correctness by achieving 100% path coverage
Use a Turing machine to enumerate all inputs
Rely on the compiler to certify the absence of bugs
Design well-designed tests that come close to being adequate

questions.explanation: The slide says only well-designed tests come close to being adequate; testing cannot guarantee complete correctness. Path coverage does not prove correctness, enumerating all inputs is infeasible, and compilers do not certify bug-freedom. (Software Testing p.5)

questions.stem: What corollary does the deck draw from Dijkstra's principle?

options.text: Residual bugs can still hide in untested code paths, undetected by any test suite
Every bug will eventually be found if tests run long enough
A green suite mathematically proves the program correct
Bugs only exist in code that has no tests at all

questions.explanation: The deck states residual bugs can still hide in the code, undetected by tests, since no suite guarantees an error-free program. The distractors overstate testing's power or wrongly claim tested code is bug-free. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the exam takeaway about a passing test suite?

options.text: It is a formal proof that the specification is satisfied
It is evidence of working features, never a proof of correctness
It guarantees no residual bugs remain in any path
It makes the Verification phase unnecessary

questions.explanation: The guide's takeaway: a passing suite is evidence of working features, never proof of correctness, which is the humility justifying Verification. The distractors overclaim or wrongly drop Verification. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: Which set of languages does the deck name as Turing-complete and therefore subject to the halting problem?
questions.explanation: The deck names JAVA, C#, C++, and C. HTML/CSS/JSON/YAML and Markdown/CSV are markup/data formats; SQL/Regex are not the cited languages; the claim is not limited to assembly. (Software Testing p.4)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say Verification 'is not about certainty'?

options.text: Because tests are run only once and then discarded
Because specifications are always correct by definition
Because the compiler already guarantees correctness
Because testing achieves systematic risk reduction, not proof, given the impossibility result

questions.explanation: The guide frames Verification as systematic risk reduction rather than certainty, following from the p.5 impossibility result. Tests are not discarded (regression re-runs them), specs can be wrong, and compilers do not guarantee correctness. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: In the basic test model, which component decides pass or fail by judging 'Outputs OK?'
questions.explanation: The oracle decides pass/fail by judging 'Outputs OK?'. The SUT produces outputs, the input generator supplies inputs, and CI orchestrates runs. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: What does the acronym SUT stand for in the test model?
questions.explanation: SUT means Software Under Test: it receives inputs and produces outputs. The other expansions are invented. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes the basic test model?

options.text: The oracle produces inputs, which feed the SUT to make outputs
The SUT calls the oracle, which then generates the inputs
Test inputs to the SUT, which produces outputs, which the oracle judges 'Outputs OK?'
Outputs feed the SUT, which produces the inputs for the oracle

questions.explanation: Inputs go into the SUT, which produces outputs, which the oracle then judges. The other orderings scramble the pipeline. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: For a typical unit test, what usually serves as the oracle?

options.text: A second human reviewer reading the output
A hard-coded expected value, e.g. assertEquals(expected, actual)

questions.explanation: For a unit test the oracle is usually a hard-coded expected value such as assertEquals(expected, actual). The distractors are not the unit-test oracle. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Besides a hard-coded expected value, which two oracle forms does the guide mention for non-unit tests?

options.text: A reference implementation (differential testing) and an invariant (checkRep())
A user interview and a stack trace
A coverage report and a profiler
A code formatter and a linter

questions.explanation: The guide notes oracles can be a reference implementation (differential testing) or an invariant (checkRep()). Interviews, stack traces, coverage reports, profilers, formatters, and linters are not oracles. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: The guide says 'choosing a correct, cheap oracle is half the art of testing.' Why does the oracle matter so much?

options.text: It determines how many CPU cores the test uses
It is the part that actually decides pass/fail, so a wrong oracle silently invalidates the test
It controls the random seed for fuzzing
It compiles the SUT before each run

questions.explanation: The oracle decides pass/fail; a wrong or expensive oracle undermines the test. It is unrelated to CPU cores, random seeds, or compilation. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Which testing level tests the smallest testable unit in isolation?
questions.explanation: Unit testing targets the smallest testable unit in isolation. Integration checks components together, system checks the whole assembly, and stress pushes load. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which testing level tests that already-unit-tested components work together across their interfaces?
questions.explanation: Integration testing checks components working together across interfaces. Unit isolates one unit, system tests the whole assembly, and random testing generates random inputs. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which testing level tests the whole assembled system end-to-end against its specification?
questions.explanation: System testing exercises the whole assembled system end-to-end against its specification. Unit and integration are narrower; differential compares against a reference. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Per TestLab1, in object-oriented programming a 'unit' under test is often what?

options.text: Always exactly one line of source code
An entire interface such as a class, though it could be an individual method

questions.explanation: TestLab1 says a unit is often an entire interface such as a class, but could be an individual method. It is not a single line, the whole app, or a DB table. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: According to TestLab1, who typically writes and runs unit tests?

options.text: A dedicated manual QA team only
Software developers, as typically automated tests

questions.explanation: TestLab1 says unit tests are typically automated tests written and run by developers. Customer-run-after-delivery describes traditional acceptance tests. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: When execution leaves the method under test during a unit test, what have you encountered and what is the strategy?

options.text: A syntax error, which you fix by recompiling
A boundary value, which you can ignore
A regression, which you roll back
A dependency, which you replace with mocks/stubs to keep a single code-path through a single method

questions.explanation: TestLab1 says leaving the method means a dependency, replaced with mocks/stubs so the test stays one code-path through one method. It is not a syntax error, boundary value, or regression. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: TDD applies to which levels of testing, according to the deck?

options.text: Unit, component, and system tests (all kinds)

questions.explanation: The deck states TDD applies to all kinds: unit, component, and system tests. Restricting it to one level contradicts the slide. (Software Testing p.35)

questions.stem: A maintainer changes a JHotDraw module. The guide says the testing levels of p.8 map onto which set?

options.text: Only the user-interface widgets, regardless of impact
The impact set: the modules touched are exactly those whose unit tests must be (re)written and run
The entire codebase, every class, every time

questions.explanation: The guide maps testing levels onto the impact set produced by Concept Location and Impact Analysis. It is not limited to UI widgets or the DB, nor does it require manually re-testing literally everything. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Why does the lecture treat the testing-kinds diagram (p.8) as having two axes rather than one list?

options.text: One axis is cost and the other is the programming language
One axis is the author's seniority and the other is the IDE
One axis is the level/scope (unit/integration/system) and the other is knowledge (white-box vs black-box)
Both axes measure code coverage percentage

questions.explanation: The p.8 map organises kinds by level/scope and by knowledge (white vs black box). Cost/language, seniority/IDE, and dual-coverage axes are not the framework. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Differential testing decides pass/fail by comparing the SUT output against what?

options.text: The number of lines of code executed
A reference implementation, e.g. an older version or alternative implementation

questions.explanation: Differential testing runs the SUT and a reference on the same inputs and asserts the outputs match; the reference is the oracle. A constant, line count, or time are not it. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Why is differential testing described as powerful in maintenance?

options.text: The previous release is a free oracle for the new one
It eliminates the need for any inputs
It proves the new version is correct

questions.explanation: In maintenance the previous release is a free oracle for the new one, a regression-flavoured idea. It still needs inputs, does not prove correctness, and does not remove dependencies. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Stress testing aims to discover what?

options.text: Whether a single input produces the correct output
Where the system breaks when pushed past expected load, volume, or rate
Whether the API signatures are stable

questions.explanation: Stress testing pushes past expected load/volume/rate to find the breaking point; it is not about single-input correctness, statement coverage, or signature stability. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Random testing works by doing what?

options.text: Randomly deleting code to see if tests still pass
Choosing a random subset of existing tests to run
Generating random inputs to explore the input space and surface unconsidered inputs
Running tests in a random order to expose ordering bugs

questions.explanation: Random testing generates random inputs to explore the input space and surface inputs the author never considered. The distractors describe mutation testing, test selection, and order randomization. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which three kinds does the p.8 diagram add that cut across the unit/integration/system levels?
questions.explanation: The p.8 diagram adds differential, stress, and random testing across levels. Coverage criteria, test doubles, and fragile-test sensitivities are separate topics. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which testing kind is most closely associated with fuzzing?
questions.explanation: The guide calls random testing fuzzing-adjacent because it generates random inputs cheaply. Differential compares implementations, stress pushes load, and unit isolates a method. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Differential testing's oracle is unusual because it is what?

options.text: A coverage threshold rather than an output check
Another implementation rather than a hard-coded expected value

questions.explanation: In differential testing the oracle is the other implementation; outputs of SUT and reference are compared. It is not a coverage threshold, exit code, or flame graph. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: In black-box testing, what does the tester have access to?

options.text: The full source code and its internal structure
Only the specification / interface, not the source code

questions.explanation: Black-box testing derives tests from the spec/interface (the contract) without seeing the code. Seeing source code is white-box; bytecode and coverage reports are not the defining view. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: In white-box (glass-box) testing, what does the tester use to design tests?

options.text: The source code's internal structure: statements, branches, paths

questions.explanation: White-box testing sees the source code and exercises its internal structure. Using only the contract is black-box; click recordings and requirements alone are not white-box. (Software Testing p.8, p.13)

questions.stem: The deck's 'Testing the Queue' slides write testEnqueueTail() to drive getTail() back to 0. Which kind of testing is this?

options.text: White-box, because it targets the internal tail wrap-around (if tail==max tail=0)
Black-box, because it only reads the public contract
Stress testing, because it pushes load
Random testing, because it uses random inputs

questions.explanation: Knowing the tail wrap-around exists and writing a test to drive it is white-box (glass-box). It is not black-box (it uses internal knowledge), nor stress or random testing. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: Which black-box design techniques does the deck identify as the dominant ones?
questions.explanation: The dominant black-box design techniques are equivalence partitioning and boundary-value analysis. Coverage criteria are white-box; mocking/spying and fault injection/stress are different topics. (Software Testing p.11-12, p.23)

questions.stem: White-box knowledge is specifically what lets you target which kind of criteria?
questions.explanation: White-box (source-level) knowledge lets you target coverage criteria. Equivalence classes come from the spec (black-box), acceptance scenarios from the user, and stress thresholds from load testing. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: What is the core idea of equivalence partitioning?

options.text: Test only the largest possible input
Run each test in a random order
Partition the input space into classes treated the same, then test one representative per class

questions.explanation: Equivalence partitioning splits the input space into classes the program should treat the same and tests one representative per class; testing more is redundant. Exhaustive, largest-only, and random-order are different ideas. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: On the 'Equivalent Tests' slide, why are 'enqueue(7); x=dequeue(); if(x==7)' and the same code with a sleep(100) in the middle in the same equivalence class?

options.text: Because the queue's contract is time-independent, so the delay should not change the result
Because both run on the same CPU
Because sleep always changes queue state

questions.explanation: They are equivalent because the queue's contract is time-independent; the sleep should not affect the outcome. Sharing a literal or a CPU is irrelevant, and sleep does not change queue state. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: Equivalence partitioning directly addresses which theoretical problem from earlier in the deck?

options.text: The impossibility of fully covering an infinite input domain (p.5)

questions.explanation: Equivalence partitioning gives adequate coverage of an infinite input domain with a finite, justified set of tests, addressing the p.5 impossibility result. The distractors are unrelated theory. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: The Equivalent Tests slide also flags 'Large Integers?' as a partition of which space?

options.text: The input space of method names
The output space, where values near the representable-range edge form their own class

questions.explanation: The slide flags 'Large Integers?' as an output-space partition: values near the edge of the representable range form their own class. It is not about method names, thread schedules, or file paths. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: Why does equivalence partitioning say testing more than one representative per class is wasteful?

options.text: Because the test framework limits the number of tests
Because the oracle can only check one value
Because inputs in the same class should be treated identically, so extra tests add no new information

questions.explanation: Within a class the program should behave the same, so additional same-class tests are redundant. Framework limits, guaranteed failure, and single-value oracles are not the reason. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: Why does boundary-value analysis focus on the edges of equivalence classes?

options.text: Because defects cluster at the edges of equivalence classes
Because edges are the only legal inputs
Because the middle of a class is untestable

questions.explanation: Defects cluster at the edges of equivalence classes, so boundaries are tested explicitly. Edges are not the only legal inputs, the middle is testable, and speed is irrelevant. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: What is the boundary-value 'rule of thumb' for a numeric/size boundary B?

options.text: Test a random value far from B

questions.explanation: The rule of thumb is to test B-1, B, and B+1 for each boundary. Testing only B, only extremes, or a far-away random value misses the cluster of defects at the edge. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: For the fixed-size queue with max=2, the run enqueue(6); enqueue(7); enqueue(8); dequeue x3 is expected to yield which tuple?

options.text: false, false, false, null, null, null

questions.explanation: The worked run yields true, true, false, 6, 7, null: two successful enqueues, a rejected third (full), then FIFO dequeues 6 and 7 and a null from the now-empty queue. The other tuples violate the size-2 contract. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: In the queue boundary run, why does the third enqueue (value 8) return false?

options.text: Because 8 is not a valid integer
Because dequeue was not called first
Because the tail wrapped around to 0
Because the queue is full (size==max==2), so enqueue must reject the element

questions.explanation: The third enqueue hits the size==max boundary, so enqueue returns false. The value's validity, dequeue ordering, and wrap-around are not why it is rejected. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: TestLab1 lists which boundary-related step as a required classwork item?

options.text: Write JUnit tests for identified boundary cases
Delete all boundary cases to speed up tests
Avoid testing empty or full states

questions.explanation: TestLab1 requires writing JUnit tests for identified boundary cases. The distractors contradict the lab's boundary requirement. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: The guide says the queue tuple 'true,true,false,6,7,null' is significant because every value is what?

options.text: A boundary outcome (empty, full-rejection, wrap-around, empty-dequeue)

questions.explanation: Every value in that expected tuple is a boundary outcome, illustrating boundary blindness if you only test best cases. They are not random, overflow, or exceptions. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Which boundary does the queue's 'if (tail == max) tail = 0;' line create?

options.text: The wrap-around boundary where tail/head hit max and reset to 0

questions.explanation: The line creates the wrap-around boundary in the ring buffer where tail/head reset to 0 at max. It is not overflow, null, or threading. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: What does statement (line) coverage require?

options.text: Every branch outcome is taken at least once
Every executable statement is run by at least one test

questions.explanation: Statement coverage requires each executable statement to run at least once and is the weakest criterion. Branch and path are stronger; trying every input is exhaustive testing. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: What does branch (decision) coverage require beyond statement coverage?

options.text: Every branch of every decision is taken, i.e. both the true and false outcome of each if
Only that every line executes once
Every loop runs an infinite number of times
Every method is called by name

questions.explanation: Branch coverage requires both true and false outcomes of each decision, which is stronger than statement coverage. Line-once is statement coverage; infinite loops and name-calling are not criteria. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: What does path coverage require?

options.text: Every independent execution path (every combination of branches) is exercised
Only that each branch's true side executes

questions.explanation: Path coverage requires every independent path (combination of branches), the strongest and usually infeasible criterion. The distractors describe weaker criteria or unrelated properties. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: Which ordering correctly ranks the coverage criteria by increasing strength?

options.text: Path subset of branch subset of statement
Branch subset of statement subset of path
Statement subset of branch subset of path
Statement subset of path subset of branch

questions.explanation: The exam framing is statement subset branch subset path in strength: higher coverage costs more tests. The other orderings invert or scramble the hierarchy. (Software Testing p.13, p.49)

questions.stem: Why is path coverage usually infeasible, and how does this relate to p.5?

options.text: Paths cannot be measured by any tool
Branch coverage already implies path coverage
Loops make the number of paths explode, the practical face of the impossibility result

questions.explanation: Loops cause the path count to explode, which is the practical face of the p.5 impossibility result; you approximate with representative paths. Paths are measurable, branch does not imply path, and statements are ordered. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: In the queue, branch coverage specifically forces which pair of enqueue tests?

options.text: One where enqueue returns false (queue full) and one where it returns true
Two tests that both return true
Two tests that both return false
One test that returns null and one that throws

questions.explanation: Branch coverage forces both the true and false outcomes of enqueue: a successful enqueue and a full-queue rejection, matching the true,true,false expected tuple. Two-same or null/throw pairs do not cover both branches. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Even at 100% statement, branch, and path coverage, why is the program still not proven correct?

options.text: Coverage measures executed structure, not the infinite set of inputs/values; missing logic and wrong oracles still hide bugs (p.6)
Because path coverage is weaker than statement coverage
Because assertions are disabled by default

questions.explanation: Full coverage exercises code structure but not every input/value, and cannot catch missing logic or a wrong oracle, so correctness is unproven (p.6). Tools can reach 100% on small code, path is the strongest, and assertion defaults are a separate concern. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: The guide grounds high statement coverage as a by-product of which practice?

options.text: TDD's rule to only write production code that makes a failing test pass

questions.explanation: Because TDD only writes production code to pass a failing test, most code is covered by construction, yielding high statement coverage. Disabling assertions, random order, and over-mocking do not produce coverage. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: A specification defines, for each input domain, what?

options.text: The number of test cases needed
The order in which tests run

questions.explanation: A specification maps each input domain to the expected output range. It does not define CPU instructions, test counts, or run order. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Which specification line captures that the square root of all reals can be complex?
questions.explanation: sqrt: R -> C maps all reals to complex, since the square root of a negative is imaginary. R+ -> R+ is non-negative reals, F+ -> F+ is non-negative floats, and F -> F+ U exception covers float-or-exception. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Which sqrt specification line shows the machine-float case that may return a value or an exception?
questions.explanation: sqrt: F -> F+ U exception covers floats including negatives, returning a float or an exception. R -> C is complex, and the R+/F+ lines are the non-negative cases. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Why does the spec slide matter for choosing which tests must exist?

options.text: It tells you which CI server to use
It sets the random seed for fuzzing
The domain-to-range table reveals the equivalence classes (positive, zero, negative, NaN, infinity, overflow) that each need a test
It decides the number of mocks

questions.explanation: The spec tells you the equivalence classes and therefore which tests must exist; black-box tests are written straight off the domain-to-range table. It is unrelated to CI servers, seeds, or mock counts. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: The spec slide contrasts real-number theory (R) with machine floats (F) to remind you of what?

options.text: Floats and reals are identical in practice
Specifications can be ignored for numeric code
Float boundaries such as overflow, NaN, and -0.0 are their own test classes

questions.explanation: R and F differ, so float boundaries (overflow, NaN, -0.0) form their own test classes. The distractors deny the distinction or the need for numeric boundary tests. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: On the spec slide, input 9 maps to which output, and input -1 maps to which set of possibilities?

options.text: 9 -> 3; -1 -> i, NaN, or exception depending on the type system

questions.explanation: The slide shows 9 -> 3 (positive root) and -1 -> i, NaN, or exception depending on the type system. The distractors give squares, wrong constants, or swapped results. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: When a test fails, the deck's decision tree says the fault could be in four places. Which is the FIRST (and most common) one to consider?

options.text: A bug in the SUT (the software under test)
A bug in the operating system

questions.explanation: The decision tree begins with a bug in the SUT, the most common case and the one you hope for because it is yours to fix. Hardware, compiler, and OS are the rarer platform category considered last. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: The fault-location decision tree lists four candidate locations. Which is the complete set, in order?
questions.explanation: The four locations are the SUT, the acceptance test/oracle, the specification, and the platform. The other lists invent categories not in the decision tree. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say 'a wrong test is worse than no test'?

options.text: A wrong test always crashes the build server
A wrong test deletes the source code
A wrong test increases coverage artificially
A bug in the test/oracle (wrong expected value or setup) gives false confidence or wastes effort 'fixing' correct code

questions.explanation: If the fault is in the test itself, you may 'fix' correct code and inject real bugs, so a wrong test is worse than none. It does not necessarily crash the build, delete code, or change coverage. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: The Mars Climate Orbiter failure is the deck's worked example of a fault in which location?

options.text: The specification/interface (metric m/s vs English ft/s units mismatch)
The SUT only, fixed by a one-line patch

questions.explanation: Mars Orbiter was a spec/interface unit mismatch (m/s vs ft/s) that no unit test caught because each side was internally consistent. It was not a pure SUT bug, a compiler issue, or an oracle seed. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: What lesson does the Mars Orbiter example teach about diagnosing failures?

options.text: Always rewrite the whole module when any test fails
Triage the fault location before 'fixing' anything, and beware integration-level unit mismatches living in the spec, not the code
Disable the failing test and move on
Trust the test over the specification every time

questions.explanation: The lesson is to triage the fault location first and watch for spec-level mismatches that no internally-consistent unit test catches. Rewriting everything, disabling tests, or blindly trusting the test are all wrong responses. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: A failing integration test results from two modules each internally consistent but disagreeing on units. Which fault location does this match?

options.text: Specification (the contract between the modules was ambiguous or wrong)

questions.explanation: When both modules are internally consistent but disagree, the fault is in the shared specification/contract, exactly the Mars Orbiter class. It is not a single SUT, the platform, or the acceptance test. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: The deck's 'Creating Testable Software' checklist triples one word for emphasis. Which word?
questions.explanation: The slide writes 'Assertions, Assertions, Assertions!!!' to stress them. Mocks, coverage, and refactoring appear elsewhere but are not the tripled word. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Which item is NOT on the design-for-testability checklist?

options.text: Add as many extra threads as possible for parallelism

questions.explanation: The checklist says NO extra threads (concurrency makes tests non-deterministic), so adding threads is the wrong answer. No global swaps, no pointer soup, and supporting fault injection are all genuine checklist items. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Why does the testability checklist forbid extra threads?

options.text: Threads always slow tests by exactly 50%

questions.explanation: Extra threads make test outcomes non-deterministic, undermining repeatability. Threads are legal, do not impose a fixed slowdown, and do not break the compiler. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Why does the testability checklist forbid swapping global variables?

options.text: Globals are deleted by the garbage collector
Hidden global state makes tests order-dependent and non-repeatable
Globals are not allowed in object-oriented code

questions.explanation: Hidden global state makes tests order-dependent and non-repeatable. The distractors misstate GC behaviour, memory, or OO rules. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: On the testability checklist, what does 'No pointer soup' protect?

options.text: Test isolation, because tangled aliasing defeats isolating a unit

questions.explanation: Tangled aliasing (pointer soup) defeats isolation, so the checklist forbids it to keep units testable in isolation. It is not about compile speed, bandwidth, or disk. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: How does the design-for-testability checklist bridge to the change process?

options.text: Prefactoring (clean before change) and Postfactoring (clean after) keep code testable, and a passing suite makes those refactorings safe
It removes the need for Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: The guide ties testability to Prefactoring and Postfactoring, with a passing suite as the safety net. It does not replace Concept Location, remove Impact Analysis, or forbid refactoring. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Which two practices does the checklist name as making code readable and therefore testable?
questions.explanation: The checklist names Clean Code and Refactor as making code readable and testable. Globals/threads and pointer soup are forbidden, and random inputs are a testing kind, not testability practices. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: How does the deck define an assertion?

options.text: A logging statement that records execution
An executable check for a property that must be true (an invariant)

questions.explanation: An assertion is an executable check for a property that must be true (an invariant). A comment is not executable, logging records rather than checks, and an assertion is not a @Test method. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Assertion Rule 1 states that assertions are NOT for what?

options.text: Error handling (expected runtime errors like bad user input)

questions.explanation: Rule 1: assertions are not for error handling; expected errors like bad input get exceptions, while assertions catch programmer mistakes ('this should never happen'). Checking mistakes, documenting invariants, and failing early are correct uses. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Per TestLab1, what is the key behavioural difference between an assertion and an exception?

options.text: An assertion should stop the program; an exception should let it continue running
An assertion logs to a file; an exception prints to console
An assertion is faster; an exception is slower
An assertion is for input; an exception is for output

questions.explanation: TestLab1 says an assertion should stop the program while an exception should let it continue. The distractors invent logging, speed, or input/output distinctions. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Assertion Rule 2 forbids what, and why?

options.text: Comments, because they clutter the code
Side effects, because disabling assertions would then change program behaviour
Multiple assertions, because only one is allowed per method
Checking invariants, because that is the oracle's job

questions.explanation: Rule 2 forbids side effects: if 'assert foo()==0' changes a global, disabling assertions changes behaviour. Comments are fine, multiple assertions are allowed, and checking invariants is exactly what assertions do. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Assertion Rule 3 says to avoid what?

options.text: Any assertion that touches an object field
Silly/trivial assertions like assert 1+1==2 that add noise, not value

questions.explanation: Rule 3 forbids silly assertions such as assert 1+1==2 that add noise without value. Touching a field, being in Java, or being in test code are not forbidden. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: The deck gives three reasons to use assertions. Which is one of them?

options.text: They replace the need for any unit tests
They speed up the program at runtime
They remove the need for a specification
They make code fail early, closer to the bug

questions.explanation: One reason is that assertions make code fail early, closer to the bug; they also make code self-checking and document assumptions. They do not replace unit tests, speed up runtime, or remove the spec. (Software Testing p.20)

questions.stem: The deck cites production assertion statistics. Which figures does it give?

options.text: GCC ~9,000 and LLVM ~13,000 assertions, about one per 110 lines of code
One assertion per 10 lines in all open-source projects
Assertions are never used in production compilers

questions.explanation: The deck states GCC ships ~9,000 and LLVM ~13,000 assertions, roughly one per 110 lines of code. The other figures are wrong or deny production use. (Software Testing p.20)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly contrasts assertions and exceptions for a bad user input?

options.text: Bad input gets an assertion; a violated invariant gets an exception
Bad input gets an exception; a violated invariant gets an assertion
Both bad input and invariants get assertions
Both bad input and invariants get exceptions

questions.explanation: Expected errors like bad input get exceptions, while a violated 'should never happen' invariant gets an assertion. The other options swap or conflate the two. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: In Java, what is the default state of assertions at runtime?

options.text: On by default; they must be explicitly disabled
Always on and cannot be disabled
Off by default; they must be enabled (e.g. java -ea)
Compiled away entirely so they never run

questions.explanation: Java assertions are off by default and enabled at runtime with -ea/-enableassertions. They are not on by default, not always on, and not necessarily compiled away. (Software Testing p.16-17)

questions.stem: What is a stated downside of disabling assertions?

options.text: You lose the safety net, because code keeps running past a violation

questions.explanation: Disabling assertions makes code run faster and keep going past a violation, but you lose the safety net. It does not block compilation, disable exceptions, or stop unit tests. (Software Testing p.17)

questions.stem: When does the deck say assertions SHOULD be enabled versus disabled?

options.text: Enable in running software that can recover by failing early; disable in a mission-critical stage where continuing is better than recovering
Always enable, even when continuing is safer

questions.explanation: The deck says enable them where failing early aids recovery, and disable in a mission-critical stage where continuing beats recovering. The absolutes and 'compilation only' answer misstate the guidance. (Software Testing p.21)

questions.stem: A common pitfall is forgetting Java assertions are off by default. What is the consequence?

options.text: The program throws AssertionError on every line
Your checkRep/invariant checks run nowhere unless you pass -ea
The compiler refuses to build the project

questions.explanation: If you never enable assertions, checkRep and invariant checks run nowhere. They do not throw everywhere, block the build, or auto-pass tests. (Software Testing p.16-17)

questions.stem: What is checkRep() in the queue example?

options.text: A method that counts test cases
A representation-invariant checker built from assertions, invoked from tests

questions.explanation: checkRep() is a representation-invariant checker built from assertions and invoked from tests (the queue is wrapped in a CheckRepWrapper). It is not a counter, a Mockito helper, or an annotation. (Software Testing p.19)

questions.stem: How does the JUnit setUp() make checkRep run on every operation in the queue example?

options.text: It calls verifyNoMoreInteractions() after each test
It wraps the queue in a CheckRepWrapper so each operation validates the invariant

questions.explanation: setUp() builds q = new CheckRepWrapper(new Queue(2)), so every operation is validated against the invariant, turning unit tests into property checks. The distractors describe Mockito, exception tests, or disabling assertions. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: The queue's checkRep encodes which invariant?

options.text: The FIFO ring-buffer invariant relating size, head, tail, and max

questions.explanation: checkRep encodes the FIFO ring-buffer invariant (size between 0 and max, with head/tail relationships). It is not a hash table, BST, or thread pool invariant. (Software Testing p.19)

questions.stem: TestLab1 requires which use of Java assertions?

options.text: Use assertions to validate user form input
Use JAVA Assertions to test invariants
Use assertions to log every method call
Use assertions instead of all exceptions

questions.explanation: TestLab1 requires using Java assertions to test invariants. Validating user input is an exception's job, assertions are not loggers, and they do not replace all exceptions. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Which assertion usage violates Rule 2 (no side effects)?

options.text: assert result > 0; after computing a real square root
assert queue.dequeue() != null; where dequeue removes an element

questions.explanation: assert queue.dequeue()!=null has a side effect (dequeue mutates the queue), so disabling assertions changes behaviour, violating Rule 2. The other three are pure invariant checks with no side effects. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: What is a fragile test?

options.text: A test that always passes regardless of the code
A test that runs only on the developer's machine
A test that breaks for reasons unrelated to a real defect, so the suite 'cries wolf'

questions.explanation: A fragile test breaks for reasons unrelated to a real defect, eroding trust in the suite. It is not an always-passing test, a machine-specific test by definition, or an assertionless test. (Software Testing p.24)

questions.stem: The deck enumerates four fragile-test 'sensitivities.' Which is the complete set?
questions.explanation: The four sensitivities are interface, behavior, data, and context. The other lists name performance metrics, test levels, or test doubles, not the sensitivities. (Software Testing p.25-28)

questions.stem: Interface sensitivity means a test is over-coupled to what?

options.text: The wall-clock time at which it runs
The shape of the API (method signatures, parameter order)

questions.explanation: Interface sensitivity couples a test to the API's shape, so renaming a method or reordering parameters breaks tests though behaviour is unchanged. Time is context, a DB row is data, and changed behaviour is behavior sensitivity. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: Behavior sensitivity differs from the other sensitivities because the breakage is often what?

options.text: Always caused by a renamed method
Correct, since the behaviour really did change (though over-specifying incidental behaviour is still noise)
Always caused by a missing database row

questions.explanation: With behavior sensitivity the breakage is correct because behaviour genuinely changed, though over-specifying incidental behaviour is noise. Clock time is context, renames are interface, and missing rows are data. (Software Testing p.26)

questions.stem: Data sensitivity means a test is over-coupled to what?

options.text: Specific data/state, such as a particular database row or fixed file

questions.explanation: Data sensitivity couples a test to specific data/state (a DB row, a fixed file); change the fixture and it breaks. Signatures are interface, time/locale are context, threads are context too. (Software Testing p.27)

questions.stem: Context sensitivity means a test is over-coupled to what?

options.text: The environment: wall-clock time, locale, file system, network, ordering

questions.explanation: Context sensitivity couples a test to the environment (time, locale, FS, network, order). Signatures are interface, a DB row is data, and changed behaviour is behavior sensitivity. (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: The Mars Climate Orbiter failure is named as an example of which fragile-test sensitivity class?
questions.explanation: The guide ties the Mars-Orbiter class to interface sensitivity (coupling to API shape/units). It is not data, behavior, or context sensitivity. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: The deck's time-dependent and fault-injection slides are cited as examples of which sensitivity?
questions.explanation: Time-dependent code and fault-injection scenarios are context-sensitivity examples (environment coupling). They are not interface, data, or behavior sensitivity. (Software Testing p.31-32)

questions.stem: What is the cross-cutting cure the deck gives for fragile tests?

options.text: Testing under the UI: pull logic out from behind the GUI and test it directly

questions.explanation: The cross-cutting cure is 'Testing under the UI', converting brittle manual tests into stable automatic ones. Deleting tests, averaging runs, and timeout tweaks are not the cure. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: How does the data-sensitivity cure relate to TestLab1?

options.text: Own and control your test data, as the lab's 'Testdata' sections do (e.g. CPR '1234651234')
Always read data from a live production database
Use random data that changes each run

questions.explanation: The cure for data sensitivity is to own and control your test data, mirrored by the lab's Testdata sections (CPR '1234651234'). Live prod data, random data, and no data all worsen fragility. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: A test fails because the build ran in a German locale where decimal separators differ. Which sensitivity is this, and what is the cure?

options.text: Context sensitivity; control the environment or inject the locale rather than depending on the ambient one
Data sensitivity; seed a database row
Behavior sensitivity; accept the new behaviour

questions.explanation: Locale dependence is context sensitivity; the cure is to control/inject the environment. Renaming (interface), seeding a row (data), and accepting changed behaviour (behavior) do not address an environment coupling. (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: Why are GUIs described as hard to test?

options.text: They have no inputs at all
They always run faster than unit tests
Their inputs are clicks, events, and swipes, and their outputs are application states, not return values

questions.explanation: GUIs are hard to test because inputs are clicks/events/swipes and outputs are application states rather than return values. They do have inputs, are not faster, and do contain logic. (Software Testing p.30)

questions.stem: What is the recommended primary strategy for testing most GUI-fronted logic?

options.text: Always drive the widgets with manual clicks
Test under the UI: call the domain/application layer directly, bypassing the widgets

questions.explanation: The primary strategy is to test under the UI, covering the bulk of logic with fast automatic tests. Manual clicking, skipping logic, and screenshot-only testing are weaker or brittle. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: Which technique captures a GUI session and replays it, and which tool does the deck name for it?
questions.explanation: Record and play captures and replays GUI sessions, with Sikuli Script named as the tool. Mockito mocks, DbUnit manages DB fixtures, and CppUnit is C++ xUnit. (Software Testing p.30, p.56)

questions.stem: Why does record-and-play GUI testing sit ABOVE the under-the-UI layer in the testing strategy?

options.text: It is faster and more reliable than unit tests
It is more fragile (context/data sensitive) and slower, so it is used sparingly on top of fast under-the-UI tests
It requires no tools or scripts
It replaces the need for domain-logic tests

questions.explanation: Record-and-play is more fragile and slower, so it sits above and is used sparingly. It is not faster/more reliable, it needs scripts/tools, and it does not replace domain-logic tests. (Software Testing p.30)

questions.stem: How does the 'test under the UI' idea map onto Clean Architecture for JHotDraw?

options.text: Put all business rules inside Swing event handlers
Test only the UI and never the domain
Keep business rules independent of the UI so they are unit-testable, and verify the thin UI layer separately and sparingly
Merge the domain and UI into one class

questions.explanation: It maps to Clean Architecture: business rules independent of the UI, unit-tested, with a thin UI verified separately. Embedding rules in handlers, UI-only testing, and merging layers all defeat testability. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: What is fault injection used for?

options.text: Removing all bugs from the SUT automatically
Causing dependency failures on demand to test unhappy paths (e.g. I/O errors, disk-full, network drops)

questions.explanation: Fault injection causes failures on demand to test error-handling paths that rarely trigger. It does not remove bugs, generate inputs, or measure coverage. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: In the deck's example, the real open() is replaced with my-open() that does what?

options.text: Succeeds 100 times, then fails 1% of calls, to deterministically test error handling
Always succeeds, to speed up tests
Opens a random file each time
Logs the call without changing behaviour

questions.explanation: my-open() succeeds 100 times then fails 1% of calls, so error-handling paths can be tested deterministically. Always-succeed, random-file, and log-only wrappers would not exercise the failure paths. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: How does the guide relate fault injection to test doubles?

options.text: Fault injection requires deleting all stubs
A stub that throws on the Nth call is fault injection in JUnit/Mockito terms
Fault injection only works with real dependencies
Fault injection is the same as random testing

questions.explanation: A stub that throws on the Nth call is fault injection expressed with test doubles. It does not require deleting stubs, need real dependencies, or equal random testing. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: Supporting fault injection appears on which deck checklist?
questions.explanation: Supporting fault injection is a design-for-testability checklist item. It is not in the Mockito limits, the assertion family, or the acceptance template. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Why is time-dependent code (timeouts, schedulers, 'is the book overdue?') hard to test?

options.text: It cannot be written in Java
It always requires a real database
It is context-sensitive and hard to test repeatably because it depends on wall-clock time

questions.explanation: Time-dependent code is context-sensitive and hard to test repeatably. It can be written in Java, does produce output, and does not always need a database. (Software Testing p.32)

questions.stem: What is the standard mitigation for testing time-dependent code?

options.text: Inject the clock: wrap time behind an interface and substitute a controllable fake/stub so 'now' is whatever the test says
Add a real sleep so tests wait for the timeout
Run the test only at midnight

questions.explanation: The mitigation is to inject the clock behind an interface and substitute a controllable fake/stub. Real sleeps, fixed run times, and disabling assertions do not make timing deterministic. (Software Testing p.32)

questions.stem: Which later deck example demonstrates the inject-the-dependency style used for time-dependent code?
questions.explanation: The Mockito 'Overdue book' example demonstrates dependency replacement for time-dependent logic. Mars Orbiter is fault triage, login is the basic JUnit pattern, and Spring shows DI. (Software Testing p.68)

questions.stem: The deck's login tests follow which structural pattern?

options.text: Act first, then arrange, then never assert
Assert twice with no action in between
Assert a precondition, perform the action, assert the postcondition (Arrange-Act-Assert)
Run the action without any assertions

questions.explanation: The login tests assert a precondition (not logged in), act (adminLogin), then assert the postcondition (logged in or still not), i.e. Arrange-Act-Assert. The distractors scramble or drop steps. (Software Testing p.33)

questions.stem: In testLoginAdmin(), what does the test assert before calling adminLogin?

options.text: That the admin is already logged in
That the admin is NOT yet logged in (the precondition)

questions.explanation: testLoginAdmin asserts assertFalse(libApp.adminLoggedIn()) as a precondition before acting. It does not assert already-logged-in, a null password, or an empty DB. (Software Testing p.33)

questions.stem: What is the defining rule of Test-Driven Development (TDD)?

options.text: Write the test before the implementation
Write the implementation, then the test

questions.explanation: TDD's defining rule is to write the test before the implementation. Writing code first is traditional testing; testing after release or not at all is not TDD. (Software Testing p.35)

questions.stem: What are the three steps of the TDD cycle, in order?
questions.explanation: The TDD cycle is red (failing test), green (minimal passing code), refactor (clean up). The other orderings misplace the steps. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: In TDD, what does the 'red' step mean?

options.text: Write production code until it compiles
Create a failing test (it fails because the feature does not exist yet)

questions.explanation: Red means creating a failing test before the feature exists. Writing production code is green-adjacent, deleting tests is neither, and refactoring is the third step. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: In TDD, what does the 'green' step mean?

options.text: Write as much code as you can imagine needing
Write only enough production code to make the failing test pass, no more

questions.explanation: Green means writing only enough production code to pass the test. Speculative code violates TDD, a second failing test is a later red, and the regression run is separate. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: In TDD, what does the 'refactor' step mean?

options.text: Clean up the code (and tests) without changing behaviour
Delete the tests once they pass
Change the behaviour to match the test

questions.explanation: Refactor means cleaning up code and tests without changing behaviour. Adding features, deleting tests, or altering behaviour are not refactoring. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: Which TDD discipline rule prevents you from over-building?

options.text: Implement every method you might ever need
Implement only as much code so that the test does not fail
Always write five tests before any code
Never run a test more than once

questions.explanation: The rule 'implement only as much code so that the test does not fail' stops over-building. Pre-building everything, fixed test counts, and single-run rules are not TDD discipline. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: How does TDD say you should grow an implementation that 'looks incomplete'?

options.text: Speculatively write the missing code without a test
Add more failing tests that force you to implement more code

questions.explanation: If a method looks incomplete you add more failing tests that force more code; you cannot write code you don't have a test for. Speculative code, deprecation, and disabling tests violate TDD. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: How many tests does TDD say you should work on at a time?

options.text: All tests for the feature simultaneously
At least three tests at once
As many as the IDE can generate

questions.explanation: TDD's discipline rule is 'one test at a time'. Working on many at once or auto-generating batches breaks the red/green/refactor rhythm. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: The deck contrasts three states of testing maturity. Which describes 'Real TDD'?

options.text: Test strictly first, before any implementation

questions.explanation: Real TDD writes the test strictly first. Code-first is Traditional Testing, the mix is 'Moving to TDD', and no tests is neither. (Software Testing p.38)

questions.stem: How does the deck define 'Traditional Testing' as opposed to TDD?
questions.explanation: Traditional Testing writes code first and tests after; TDD writes the test first. The other options are not the contrast drawn. (Software Testing p.36)

questions.stem: In the deck's 'ideas for tests', use-case scenarios (missing functions) map to which test type?
questions.explanation: Use-case scenarios (missing functions) map to acceptance tests. Defects map to defect tests, complex class behaviour to unit tests, and stress is a separate kind. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: In the deck's 'ideas for tests', a possibility for defects (missing code) maps to which test type?
questions.explanation: A possibility for defects (missing code) maps to defect tests, which pin down where you might write more code than needed. Acceptance, unit, and random tests come from other idea sources. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: In the deck's 'ideas for tests', complex behaviour of classes maps to which test type?
questions.explanation: Complex behaviour of classes maps to unit tests. Use cases map to acceptance tests, defect possibilities to defect tests, and stress is separate. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: What is the rationale the deck gives for a 'defect test'?

options.text: Defect tests delete buggy code automatically
You may want to write more code than necessary to pass a test, so a defect test pins that extra behaviour down

questions.explanation: The deck says you want to write more code than necessary, so a defect test pins it down. Defect tests do not delete code, measure coverage, or replace acceptance tests. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: Which of these is listed as an 'idea for tests' source in the deck?

options.text: Code experiments ('How does the system behave, if ...')

questions.explanation: Code experiments ('How does the system behave, if ...') is a listed source of test ideas, alongside making a list of new test ideas. The metrics in the distractors are not test-idea sources. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: In the Borrow Book use case, what is the alternative scenario?

options.text: The user has no library card, so login fails
The book is missing from the catalogue
The user already has 10 books borrowed, so the system shows an error

questions.explanation: The alternative scenario is the user already having 10 books borrowed, triggering an error. No-card, missing-book, and network-down are not the deck's alternative scenario. (Software Testing p.41)

questions.stem: In testBorrowBook(), what test data does the deck define?

options.text: A user CPR '1234651234' and a book signature 'Som001'

questions.explanation: The Borrow Book test data is CPR '1234651234' and signature 'Som001'. admin/adminadmin is the login example, /tmp/foo is fault injection, and 43 is the Mockito getUniqueId stub. (Software Testing p.42)

questions.stem: In testBorrowBook(), what is asserted as the precondition before user.borrowBook(book)?

options.text: assertTrue(borrowedBooks.contains(book)) — the book is already borrowed
assertFalse(borrowedBooks.contains(book)) — the book is not yet borrowed

questions.explanation: The precondition asserts the book is not yet in the borrowed list. Asserting it is already borrowed, a size of 10, or a null user are not the precondition. (Software Testing p.43)

questions.stem: What is the minimal 'green' implementation of borrowBook for the main scenario?

options.text: public void borrowBook(Book book) { throw new TooManyBooksException(); }
public void borrowBook(Book book) { if (book == null) return; }

questions.explanation: The minimal green implementation just adds the book to the list. Throwing always, a null-guard alone, or clearing the list would not pass the main-scenario test. (Software Testing p.44)

questions.stem: After the main scenario passes, what is the alternative-scenario test, and what does it expect?

options.text: Borrow 1 book and expect a NullPointerException
Log in as admin and expect true
Open a file and expect it to fail 1% of the time
Borrow 10 books then try to borrow 'Som001' and expect a TooManyBooksException

questions.explanation: The alternative test borrows 10 books, then expects a TooManyBooksException on the eleventh. The distractors describe other deck examples (null, login, fault injection). (Software Testing p.45)

questions.stem: What is the 'green' implementation that satisfies the alternative scenario?

options.text: Add a guard: if (borrowedBooks.size() >= 10) throw new TooManyBooksException(); before adding
Return early when book is null

questions.explanation: The alternative implementation guards with if size>=10 throw TooManyBooksException before adding. Removing the list, swallowing exceptions, or only null-guarding would not satisfy the test. (Software Testing p.46)

questions.stem: The deck's final Borrow Book test asks 'What happens if book==null?' What does it check?

options.text: That the borrowed count did not change (a boundary/defect test)
That the user is logged out
That the book is added twice

questions.explanation: The null test checks that the borrowed count did not change, a boundary/defect test. It is not about TooManyBooksException, logout, or double-adding. (Software Testing p.47)

questions.stem: How does the Borrow Book example demonstrate that TDD 'grows' an implementation?

options.text: It writes the full implementation first and tests last
It uses random inputs to generate code
Each new failing test (alternative path, null case) forces additional code that the previous code did not have
It refactors before writing any test

questions.explanation: Each new red (alternative path, null case) forces more code, growing the implementation incrementally. It does not code-first, generate code randomly, or refactor before testing. (Software Testing p.41-47)

questions.stem: Why is refactoring called the mandatory third step of TDD?

options.text: It is optional and usually skipped
It adds new features to the system
It restructures the system without changing functionality to improve the design, and it is a necessary step

questions.explanation: Refactoring is the necessary third step: restructure without changing functionality to improve design. It is not optional, not feature-adding, and not test-deleting. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: Refactoring in TDD requires what to be safe?

options.text: A good test suite, because the tests prove the restructuring preserved behaviour

questions.explanation: Refactoring requires a good test suite that proves behaviour is preserved. Disabled assertions, fresh databases, and pair watching are not the stated requirement. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: Which principle does the deck cite as a refactoring goal in TDD?

options.text: DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself; remove code duplication

questions.explanation: The deck names removing duplication via the DRY principle as a refactoring goal. WET is the opposite, and the other expansions misstate the principles. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: In change-process terms, what does the guide say green tests are?

options.text: A reason to skip Impact Analysis
The licence to refactor; without them Prefactoring and Postfactoring are unsafe

questions.explanation: Green tests are the licence to refactor; without them Prefactoring and Postfactoring are unsafe. They do not replace Concept Location, skip Impact Analysis, or obstruct refactoring. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: Which is a stated 'test benefit' of TDD?

options.text: Good code coverage, because you only write production code to make a failing test pass

questions.explanation: A test benefit is good code coverage by construction: only write production code to pass a failing test. Slower tests, more globals, and bigger methods are not benefits. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: Which is a stated 'design benefit' of TDD?

options.text: It helps design the system by defining its usage before implementation, yielding a testable system
It removes the need for any design
It forces you to use inheritance
It guarantees the absence of bugs

questions.explanation: A design benefit is that TDD defines system usage (the API from the caller's view) before implementation and yields a testable system. It does not remove design, mandate inheritance, or guarantee bug-freedom. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: Why does TDD produce high code coverage 'by construction'?

options.text: Because the IDE auto-generates tests for every line
Because no production code is written except to pass a failing test, so almost all code is reached by some test
Because coverage tools insert missing tests

questions.explanation: Coverage is high by construction because production code only exists to pass a failing test, so a test reaches it. IDE auto-generation, tool insertion, and refactoring-adds-tests are not the mechanism. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: What is JUnit, according to the deck?

options.text: A Java build tool that compiles code
A framework for writing tests, part of the xUnit family

questions.explanation: JUnit is a framework for writing tests in the xUnit family. It is not a build tool, a linter, or a VCS. (Software Testing p.51)

questions.stem: Who wrote JUnit, according to the deck?
questions.explanation: JUnit was written by Erich Gamma (a Design Patterns author) and Kent Beck (XP/TDD). Fowler/Martin, Dijkstra/Turing, and 'all four GoF' are incorrect attributions. (Software Testing p.51)

questions.stem: Why is the JUnit authorship relevant to the JHotDraw case study?

options.text: Erich Gamma co-authored both JUnit and JHotDraw, so JHotDraw's interface-based design is ideal for mocks/stubs
JHotDraw is written in C++ tested with CppUnit

questions.explanation: Erich Gamma co-authored JUnit and JHotDraw, and JHotDraw's pattern-based, interface-based design suits mocks/stubs. JHotDraw is Java/Swing, not C++, Beck did not design its GUI, and it is full of patterns. (Software Testing p.51)

questions.stem: Which test types does the deck say JUnit supports?
questions.explanation: JUnit supports unit, component, and acceptance tests. It is not limited to units, screenshots, or stress tests. (Software Testing p.51)

questions.stem: What does the @Test annotation do in JUnit?

options.text: Marks a method as a test method
Groups several test classes into a suite

questions.explanation: @Test marks a method as a test. Once-before-all is @BeforeClass, after-each is @After, and grouping is @RunWith(Suite.class). (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: How often do @Before and @After methods run in JUnit 4?

options.text: Once before and after all tests in the class
Before and after each test method

questions.explanation: @Before/@After run before/after each test method, giving a fresh fixture per test. Once-per-class is @BeforeClass/@AfterClass; they do not run only on failure or at JVM startup. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: How often do @BeforeClass and @AfterClass methods run, and what must they be?

options.text: Once before/after all tests in the class, and they must be static
Before/after each test, and they must be private
Once per assertion, and they must be final

questions.explanation: @BeforeClass/@AfterClass run once per class for expensive shared setup and must be static. They are not per-test, per-assertion, or deprecated. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Which JUnit annotation is appropriate for expensive shared setup such as opening a database connection?
questions.explanation: @BeforeClass runs once and is right for expensive shared setup like opening a DB. @Before runs per test (wasteful for a shared DB), @Test marks tests, and @After tears down per test. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: In the EnqueueTest example, what does setUp() (a @Before method) build, and why?

options.text: A Mockito mock of the database
q = new CheckRepWrapper(new Queue(2)); so every test starts with a clean, invariant-checked queue

questions.explanation: setUp() builds a fresh CheckRepWrapper around a Queue(2) so each test starts clean and invariant-checked. It is not a mock, a Spring context, or a thread pool. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: In the EnqueueTest example, what does tearDown() (a @After method) do?

options.text: Closes the database once for all tests
Creates the queue before each test
Empties the queue after each test

questions.explanation: tearDown() empties the queue after each test. Closing a DB once is @AfterClass, creating the queue is setUp(), and verifying mocks is Mockito. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: In the EnqueueTest, after enqueueing 1, 2, then 3 into a Queue(2), what does the test assert and why?

options.text: assertTrue(q.getTail() == 0) — the tail wrapped around to 0 at the max boundary

questions.explanation: The test asserts getTail()==0 because the wrap-around boundary resets tail to 0. The queue cannot hold 3, is not nulled, and the assertion targets tail, not head. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Which import brings in JUnit's assertion methods?
questions.explanation: JUnit assertions come from 'import static org.junit.Assert.*;'. There is no java.util.Assert, Mockito is for mocks, and org.junit.Test only brings the annotation. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: Which is a JUnit assertion method listed in the deck?
questions.explanation: assertEquals(exp, act) is a listed JUnit assertion. The other names are invented and not part of JUnit's API. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: Why does the deck recommend specialised assertions like assertEquals or assertNull over a plain assertTrue?

options.text: They are the only ones allowed in JUnit 4

questions.explanation: Specialised assertions give clearer failure messages. They are not faster, not the only allowed forms, and do not disable assertions. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: Which JUnit assertion forces a test to fail unconditionally when reached?
questions.explanation: fail() forces an unconditional failure (used e.g. when no exception was thrown). assertTrue(true), assertNotNull, and assertEquals(1,1) would pass. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: How do you group several test classes into a JUnit test suite?
questions.explanation: A suite uses @RunWith(Suite.class) and @Suite.SuiteClasses({...}). Lifecycle annotations, exception annotations, and Mockito annotations do not build suites. (Software Testing p.54)

questions.stem: What does @Suite.SuiteClasses({EnqueueTest.class, DequeueTest.class}) accomplish?

options.text: Runs both test classes together as one suite

questions.explanation: It runs EnqueueTest and DequeueTest together as a suite. It does not mock, assert exceptions, or disable assertions. (Software Testing p.54)

questions.stem: Which JUnit idiom concisely asserts that a method throws a specific exception type?

options.text: assertThrows is the only way in JUnit 4

questions.explanation: The concise annotation idiom is @Test(expected = MyException.class). 'throws=' and @ExpectException are invented; the deck shows the annotation and the try/fail/catch idiom, not assertThrows. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: When should you prefer the try/fail/catch idiom over @Test(expected=...)?

options.text: When you must inspect/assert on the exception's contents
When you only care about the exception type
When you want fewer lines of code

questions.explanation: Use try/fail/catch when you must assert on the exception's contents; use the annotation when only the type matters. The other options misuse the idioms. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: In the try/fail/catch exception idiom, what is the role of fail()?

options.text: It logs the exception to a file
It fails the test if no exception was thrown (the catch is skipped only when m() threw)
It marks the test as ignored

questions.explanation: fail() runs only if m() did NOT throw, failing the test; if m() throws, control jumps to catch and fail() is skipped. It does not log, re-throw, or ignore. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: Which deck-listed tool is the C++ member of the xUnit family?
questions.explanation: CppUnit is the C++ xUnit tool. Mockito mocks, Sikuli Script does GUI image-based testing, and DbUnit manages database fixtures. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: Which deck-listed tool manages database fixtures?
questions.explanation: DbUnit manages database fixtures. CppUnit is C++ xUnit, Sikuli does GUI image testing, and Mockito mocks. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: Which deck-listed tool performs GUI image-based testing?
questions.explanation: Sikuli Script does GUI image-based (record-and-play) testing. DbUnit is for DB fixtures, CppUnit is C++ xUnit, and Mockito mocks. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: In the Spring + JUnit example, how is the DataModelService obtained in the test?

options.text: It is created with new DataModelService()
It is injected by Spring via @Autowired @Qualifier("ml")
It is read from a file

questions.explanation: The bean is injected by Spring with @Autowired @Qualifier("ml"). It is not new-ed, mocked, or file-loaded in that example. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: What runner and configuration does the Spring + JUnit test use?
questions.explanation: The Spring test uses SpringJUnit4ClassRunner and @ContextConfiguration(classes={AppConfig.class}). Suite, MockitoJUnitRunner, and no-runner are different setups. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: The Spring + JUnit example uses assertThat(...).is(...)/instanceOf(...). What library provides these matchers?
questions.explanation: assertThat with is()/instanceOf() are Hamcrest matchers for readable assertions. They are not from Mockito, JUnit's Assert, or Spring Data. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: What is a test double, in general?

options.text: A stand-in for a real dependency in a test
A test that runs twice for reliability
A duplicate copy of the production database
A second assertion in a test method

questions.explanation: A test double is a stand-in for a real dependency, keeping the test isolated, fast, and deterministic. It is not a double run, a DB copy, or a second assertion. (Software Testing p.57-60)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a stub?

options.text: A double that records and verifies interactions
An object holding predefined data to provide responses during tests; the lightest and most static double
A partial mock wrapping a real object

questions.explanation: A stub holds predefined data and answers queries with canned values; it is the lightest, most static double. Recording/verifying is a mock, partial-real is a spy, and working-simplified is a fake. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a mock?

options.text: An object holding only predefined data with no verification
A real object with one method overridden
An object that stores method calls and lets you record and verify interactions; the most powerful and flexible double

questions.explanation: A mock stores method calls and verifies interactions; it is the most powerful and flexible double. Canned-data-only is a stub, real-with-override is a spy, and in-memory-simplified is a fake. (Software Testing p.59)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a spy?

options.text: A double with no real behaviour at all
A framework that generates random inputs
A partial mock wrapping a real object, delegating every call unless specified otherwise

questions.explanation: A spy is a partial mock wrapping a real object; by default it delegates to the real object and you override/verify a few methods. The distractors describe a full mock, a stub, and a fuzzer. (Software Testing p.72-73)

questions.stem: By default, what happens to a call on a Mockito spy that has not been explicitly stubbed?

options.text: It is delegated to the real wrapped object
It is recorded but never executed

questions.explanation: Every spy call, unless specified otherwise, is delegated to the real object. It does not return null, throw, or skip execution by default. (Software Testing p.73)

questions.stem: Which double should you choose when the interaction (that a method was called) is the thing under test?

options.text: A stub, because it only returns canned data
A mock, because it records and verifies interactions
A spy, because it always delegates
A fake, because it is a full implementation

questions.explanation: A mock is chosen when you must verify interactions; a stub does not verify, a spy mostly delegates, and a fake is a working implementation without interaction verification as its purpose. (Software Testing p.59)

questions.stem: Which double should you choose for a pure query where you only need a canned answer and do NOT need to verify calls?
questions.explanation: For a pure query needing only a canned value, a stub suffices; reaching for a mock over-specifies the test. A spy wraps a real object, and @InjectMocks is a wiring annotation, not a double. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: Why does picking a mock when a stub would do make tests more fragile?

options.text: Mocks run slower than stubs by design
Mocks add interaction verification, so the test over-specifies incidental calls and breaks on harmless changes

questions.explanation: A mock verifies interactions, so using one where a stub suffices over-specifies the test and makes it brittle. Mocks are not inherently slow, can return values, and do not need a real DB. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly orders the doubles from lightest/most-static to most-powerful, per the deck?

options.text: Stub, mock; with spy being a partial-real hybrid
Mock, stub; with spy being fully fake
Spy, stub, mock all equally static
Fake, dummy, mock in that strength order

questions.explanation: The deck calls the stub the lightest/most static and the mock the most powerful/flexible, with the spy a partial-real hybrid. The other orderings misrank or mislabel the doubles. (Software Testing p.59-60, p.72)

questions.stem: The guide notes two doubles beyond mock/stub/spy for exam completeness. Which are they?

options.text: Fakes (working but simplified implementations) and dummies (objects passed but never used)

questions.explanation: The broader taxonomy adds fakes (working but simplified, e.g. in-memory DB) and dummies (passed but unused). Proxies, adapters/decorators, and singletons/factories are patterns, not doubles. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: Which test double is a working but simplified implementation, e.g. an in-memory database?
questions.explanation: A fake is a working but simplified implementation such as an in-memory database. A stub returns canned data, a mock verifies interactions, and a dummy is passed but unused. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: What is Mockito?

options.text: A popular mock framework used with JUnit to create and configure mock objects
