questions.explanation: Mockito is a mock framework used with JUnit to create and configure mocks. It is not a compiler, a GUI tool (that is Sikuli), or a coverage tool. (Software Testing p.58)

questions.stem: Which two ways does the deck give to create a Mockito mock?

options.text: The static Mockito.mock(MyClass.class) method, or the @Mock annotation

questions.explanation: You create a mock with Mockito.mock(MyClass.class) or the @Mock annotation. new Mock()/Mock.create() do not exist, @Test/@Before are lifecycle, and @Spy/@InjectMocks serve other roles. (Software Testing p.65)

questions.stem: When using the @Mock annotation, what call must you make to initialize the annotated fields?
questions.explanation: With annotations you must call MockitoAnnotations.initMocks(this), or the annotated fields stay null. reset(), a constructor call, and verify() do not initialize annotations. (Software Testing p.65)

questions.stem: Which Mockito construct stubs a return value for a condition (fluent API)?
questions.explanation: when(...).thenReturn(...) stubs a return value (doReturn(obj).when(...) works similarly). verify().times() checks calls, assertEquals is JUnit, and initMocks initializes annotations. (Software Testing p.69-70)

questions.stem: In the example when(test.getUniqueId()).thenReturn(43), what is the effect?

options.text: It verifies getUniqueId was called once
It throws if getUniqueId is called
Calling test.getUniqueId() on the mock returns 43

questions.explanation: It stubs the mock so getUniqueId() returns 43. It is not a verification, does not throw, and does not delete the method. (Software Testing p.70)

questions.stem: Which Mockito construct asserts that a method was actually called?
questions.explanation: verify() asserts a method was called (optionally with matchers and counts). when().thenReturn() stubs returns, mock() creates a mock, and @InjectMocks wires dependencies. (Software Testing p.69)

questions.stem: What does Mockito.verify(test, Mockito.times(2)) check?

options.text: That the method was called exactly twice
That the test runs for 2 seconds

questions.explanation: verify(test, times(2)) checks the method was called exactly twice. It is not about return values, mock counts, or timing. (Software Testing p.71)

questions.stem: What does Mockito.verify(test).testing(Matchers.eq(12)) assert?

options.text: That testing() was called (once) with the argument 12

questions.explanation: It asserts testing() was called once with argument 12, using an argument matcher. It is not about return values, mock counts, or throwing. (Software Testing p.71)

questions.stem: What does verifyNoMoreInteractions() check?

options.text: That the test threw an exception
That no other (unverified) method was called on the mock

questions.explanation: verifyNoMoreInteractions() checks that no other method was called beyond those already verified. It is not about null returns, stub usage, or exceptions. (Software Testing p.74)

questions.stem: What does @InjectMocks do?

options.text: Tries constructor dependency injection by type, wiring @Mock/@Spy fields into the object under test
Marks a method as a test

questions.explanation: @InjectMocks does constructor dependency injection by type, wiring @Mock/@Spy fields into the object under test. It is not @Test, a Spring context, or an assertion switch. (Software Testing p.74)

questions.stem: What does @Spy do in Mockito?

options.text: Creates a fully fake object with no real behaviour
Wraps a real object so most calls are real but you can override/verify a few

questions.explanation: @Spy wraps a real object, delegating by default. A fully fake object is @Mock, injection is @InjectMocks, and stubbing is when().thenReturn(). (Software Testing p.73)

questions.stem: In the ArticleManagerTest example, which fields are mocks and which is a spy?

options.text: calculator and database are @Spy; userProvider is @Mock
calculator and database are @Mock; userProvider is @Spy

questions.explanation: In the example, calculator and database are @Mock and userProvider is @Spy (wrapping a real ConsumerUserProvider), with manager as @InjectMocks. The other groupings misread the annotations. (Software Testing p.75)

questions.stem: In the ArticleManagerTest, what does verify(database).addListener(any(ArticleListener.class)) assert?

options.text: That the database is a real object
That addListener was called on the database mock with some ArticleListener argument

questions.explanation: It verifies addListener was called with any ArticleListener argument. It is not a return check, a real-object claim, or a no-call assertion. (Software Testing p.75)

questions.stem: Which constructs can Mockito NOT mock?

options.text: Final classes, anonymous classes, and primitive types
Interfaces, abstract classes, and public methods

questions.explanation: Mockito cannot mock final classes, anonymous classes, or primitive types. Interfaces and abstract classes are mockable, and the other options misstate the limits. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: What design implication follows from Mockito's inability to mock final/anonymous classes?

options.text: Always declare classes final for safety
Program to interfaces and avoid final on classes you need to mock

questions.explanation: The implication is to program to interfaces and avoid final on classes you must mock. Declaring everything final, using anonymous classes, or avoiding interfaces would worsen mockability. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: A common pitfall is forgetting MockitoAnnotations.initMocks(this) when using @Mock/@Spy/@InjectMocks. What goes wrong?

options.text: The mock becomes a real object

questions.explanation: Without initMocks(this), the annotated fields stay null. It does not double-run the test, disable assertions, or turn the mock real. (Software Testing p.65, p.75)

questions.stem: The Mockito problem/DataServer/Mock sequence illustrates which principle?

options.text: You must always test against the production server
Mocks replace the need for JUnit
You stand up a fake data source so the unit test never touches a real server

questions.explanation: The DataServer sequence shows standing up a fake data source so the unit test never hits a real server. It is the opposite of testing against production, and mocks neither measure coverage nor replace JUnit. (Software Testing p.61-66)

questions.stem: In the 'Overdue book' mock example, how is the external OverdueService handled in the unit test?

options.text: It is replaced with a Mockito mock stubbed with when(svc.hasOverdue(user)).thenReturn(false)
It is called against the live network
It is removed from the code permanently
It is tested with a real clock and sleep

questions.explanation: The OverdueService is replaced by a Mockito mock stubbed to return false, so the test never hits the network, and verify(svc).hasOverdue(user) checks the interaction. Live network, permanent removal, and real sleeps defeat isolation. (Software Testing p.68)

questions.stem: How does the deck define an acceptance test?

options.text: A test of a single private method in isolation
A test defined by or with the help of the user, based on the requirements
A test that pushes the system past expected load
A test that compares two implementations

questions.explanation: An acceptance test is defined with the user, based on requirements. Single-method isolation is unit testing, load is stress testing, and comparing implementations is differential testing. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: How are acceptance tests run 'traditionally'?

options.text: Automatic tests, written before the story, run by developers
Random fuzzing run by the CI server
Manual tests, run by the customer, after delivery, based on use cases/user stories
Differential tests against the previous release

questions.explanation: Traditionally, acceptance tests are manual, run by the customer after delivery, based on use cases. The automatic-before-story form is the Agile mode; fuzzing and differential testing are other kinds. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: How are acceptance tests done in the 'Agile' mode?

options.text: Manual tests run only after delivery
Automatic tests (JUnit, Fit) created before the user story is implemented

questions.explanation: In Agile, acceptance tests are automatic (JUnit, Fit) and created before the story is implemented (the red-first discipline at feature level). Manual-after-delivery is the traditional mode; stress and reviews are not acceptance tests. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: The deck's acceptance-test template (Login Admin) includes which fields?

options.text: Name, Actor, Precondition, Main scenario, Alternative scenarios, Postcondition

questions.explanation: The template lists Name, Actor, Precondition, Main scenario, Alternative scenarios, Postcondition. The other lists are doubles, the TDD cycle, and coverage criteria. (Software Testing p.79)

questions.stem: How does the acceptance-test template bridge to BDD/Verification?

options.text: The acceptance scenario IS the specification of 'done', and automating it confirms the feature meets the user's requirement
It only documents the GUI layout

questions.explanation: The acceptance scenario is the spec of 'done', and automating it is how Verification confirms the feature meets the requirement, the BDD bridge. It does not replace unit tests, document layout, or measure coverage. (Software Testing p.79)

questions.stem: In the Login Admin acceptance template, what is the Precondition and Postcondition?

options.text: Precondition: Admin logged in; Postcondition: Admin logged out
Precondition: database empty; Postcondition: database full
Precondition: Admin not logged in; Postcondition: Admin logged in
Precondition: book borrowed; Postcondition: book returned

questions.explanation: The template's precondition is 'Admin not logged in' and postcondition is 'Admin logged in'. The other pairs come from different scenarios. (Software Testing p.79)

questions.stem: What is the deck's verdict on manual tests?

options.text: Manual tests are cheaper than automated ones
Manual tests give immediate regression feedback
Manual tests are required for unit testing
Manual tests should be avoided because they are expensive and can't be run often

questions.explanation: The deck says manual tests should be avoided: expensive in time and personnel and can't run often. They are not cheaper, do not give immediate regression feedback, and are not required for unit testing. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Why are automated tests preferred over manual tests?

options.text: They are cheap to execute and can run as soon as something changes, giving immediate feedback
They never need to be written
They eliminate the need for a specification
They prove the absence of bugs

questions.explanation: Automated tests are cheap to run and can run on every change, giving immediate feedback. They still must be written, do not remove the spec, and do not prove correctness. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: What is regression testing?

options.text: Re-running the existing automated suite after each change to confirm nothing previously working broke
Comparing two implementations on random inputs

questions.explanation: Regression testing re-runs the automated suite after each change to catch new breakage. New-feature-only, load, and implementation-comparison describe other testing kinds. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: What enables regression testing, according to the deck?

options.text: Manual tests run by the customer
Automated tests, because they can run cheaply as soon as something changes

questions.explanation: Automation enables regression testing because automated tests run cheaply on every change. Manual tests, disabling assertions, and removing dependencies do not enable regression. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Why is regression testing called the heartbeat of Verification and CI?

options.text: It runs only once at the end of the project
It replaces the need for unit tests
Every commit re-runs the suite, giving immediate feedback if a change introduced a bug

questions.explanation: Regression is the heartbeat of Verification/CI because every commit re-runs the suite for immediate feedback. It is not a one-time end-of-project step, does not replace unit tests, and is not UI-only. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: What does the deck say is the remaining difficulty in regression testing, and the solution?

options.text: The UI is more difficult (but not impossible); the solution is to test under the UI
Unit tests are impossible to automate; write them manually
Assertions slow it down; disable them
Databases cannot be tested; skip them

questions.explanation: The remaining difficulty is the UI (more difficult but not impossible), solved by testing under the UI. The other options misstate the difficulty or solution. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: How does the guide connect automated regression to the change process steps Postfactoring and Actualization?

options.text: Because they happen continuously, only an automated regression suite makes them safe — the suite is the safety net for ongoing change
Because Actualization requires manual tests only

questions.explanation: The guide says continuous Postfactoring and Actualization are only safe with an automated regression suite as the safety net. Regression does not replace Actualization, Postfactoring does not disable the suite, and manual tests are discouraged. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: What is the tester's mindset, contrasted with the developer's, on the closing slide?

options.text: Developer: 'I want this code to fail'; Tester: 'I want this code to succeed'
Both want the code to compile only
Developer: 'I want this code to succeed'; Tester: 'I want this code to fail'

questions.explanation: The slide frames the developer wanting success and the tester wanting failure. The inverted 'developer wants failure, tester wants success' wording is wrong, and the 'compile only' and 'coverage only' framings miss the cultural contrast. (Software Testing p.83)

questions.stem: Who is the 'ideal' engineer in the testing-mindset slide?

options.text: The developer who only wants success
The tester who only wants failure
The Hybrid who holds both the developer's and the tester's mindset

questions.explanation: The ideal is the Hybrid who holds both mindsets. A pure developer or pure tester is one-sided, and the manager is not in the framing. (Software Testing p.83)

questions.stem: Which practical advice does the tester-mindset slide give?

options.text: Test creatively and don't ignore the weird stuff
Avoid edge cases to save time
Trust the developer's claims without testing

questions.explanation: The slide advises testing creatively and not ignoring the weird stuff, because off-nominal observations reveal real bugs. The other options contradict good testing. (Software Testing p.83)

questions.stem: For testing a JHotDraw (Swing) feature, what does TestLab1 recommend regarding JUnit version?

options.text: Create JUnit 4 tests for domain logic, because Swing and JUnit extensions often work best with JUnit 4
Avoid JUnit and use only manual tests

questions.explanation: TestLab1 recommends JUnit 4 for domain-logic tests because Swing and JUnit extensions often work best with JUnit 4. JUnit 5-only, manual-only, and TestNG are not the lab's guidance. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: How should you test a JHotDraw figure's behaviour, given the deck's GUI advice?

options.text: Drive the Swing widgets with recorded clicks for all logic
Only take screenshots of the canvas
Test the domain logic of figures/handles/commands by calling the model directly with JUnit, not by driving Swing widgets
Skip testing figures because GUIs cannot be tested

questions.explanation: You test JHotDraw domain logic by calling the model directly with JUnit (test under the UI), since GUI inputs/outputs are brittle. Widget-driving, screenshot-only, and skipping are weaker or wrong. (Software Testing p.29-30)

questions.stem: When a JHotDraw collaborator (e.g. a drawing context or storage layer) is a dependency, what does the guide recommend?

options.text: Always use the real network and disk
Make the class final to ease testing
Replace it with mocks/stubs, since JHotDraw's interface-based design suits them

questions.explanation: JHotDraw's interface-based design suits mocks/stubs for dependencies like a drawing context or storage layer. Real network/disk, making classes final (which blocks Mockito), and avoiding tests are wrong. (Software Testing p.57-75)

questions.stem: How do assertions/checkRep translate to JHotDraw?

options.text: As invariant checks on figure bounds, connection endpoints, or drawing z-order, per TestLab1's 'test invariants' requirement
As logging statements for every mouse click
As replacements for all exceptions in the GUI

questions.explanation: checkRep translates to JHotDraw invariants like figure bounds, connection endpoints, or z-order, encoded as asserts. They are not click loggers, exception replacements, or coverage counters. (Software Testing p.18-19)

questions.stem: What is the portfolio deliverable that ties Lecture 7 to the JHotDraw feature, per TestLab1?

options.text: A screenshot of the running GUI only
Unit tests of important business functionality of your selected feature, with documentation of how you verified it
A list of all bugs in JHotDraw
A rewrite of the entire JHotDraw codebase

questions.explanation: The portfolio deliverable is unit tests of the feature's business functionality plus documentation of how you verified it. A screenshot, a bug list, and a full rewrite are not the deliverable. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Which phase of Rajlich's change mini-cycle is Lecture 7 (testing) about?
questions.explanation: Testing is the engine of the Verification phase, which proves the change does what it should and broke nothing. Concept Location, Impact Analysis, and Initiation are earlier phases. (Software Testing p.1)

questions.stem: In the change mini-cycle, where does Verification sit relative to Actualization and Postfactoring?

options.text: After Actualization and Postfactoring (Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion, Verification)

questions.explanation: Verification comes after Actualization and Postfactoring in the mini-cycle. It is not before Concept Location, not between Initiation and Concept Location, and not the same as Prefactoring. (Software Testing p.1)

questions.stem: A synthesis exam question asks why a green regression suite makes Prefactoring and Postfactoring safe. What is the best answer?

options.text: The suite proves behaviour is unchanged, so restructuring before/after a change cannot silently break functionality
The suite generates the refactored code automatically
The suite removes the need for Concept Location
The suite proves the absence of all bugs

questions.explanation: A green suite proves behaviour is preserved, making before/after restructuring safe. It does not generate code, replace Concept Location, or prove absence of all bugs. (Software Testing p.48, p.81)

questions.stem: In the worked-example walkthrough, what is Step 9 (the regression run) meant to confirm?

options.text: That only the new test passes
That the whole suite (this feature plus all prior features) is green, so the change introduced no regression

questions.explanation: Step 9 runs the whole suite; green everywhere means no regression and the change is verified. A single-test pass, disabled assertions, and GUI rendering are not the goal. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: If the Step 9 regression run goes red somewhere, what does the walkthrough say to do?

options.text: Disable the failing test and ship
Triage via the p.9 decision tree (SUT? test? spec? platform?) before fixing

questions.explanation: A red regression run triggers fault triage via the p.9 decision tree before fixing. Rewriting everything, disabling and shipping, or assuming hardware are wrong responses. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: What does the halting problem make theoretically impossible regarding tests?
questions.explanation: The halting problem makes a perfect, complete test suite theoretically impossible. It does not prevent writing a unit test, compiling, or running JUnit. (Software Testing p.4-5)

questions.stem: What is the test oracle responsible for?

options.text: Deciding pass/fail by judging whether outputs are OK

questions.explanation: The oracle decides pass/fail. Input generation, compilation, and mocking are separate concerns. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Black-box tests are derived from what?
questions.explanation: Black-box tests come from the spec/interface (domain to range). Statements/branches are white-box, and bytecode/registers are irrelevant to test derivation. (Software Testing p.8, p.23)

questions.stem: White-box tests are derived from what?
questions.explanation: White-box tests come from source-code structure. Interface-only is black-box, and clicks/requirements alone are neither. (Software Testing p.8, p.13)

questions.stem: What does equivalence partitioning split, and how many tests per class?

options.text: The code into modules; ten tests per module
The team into pairs; two tests each
The schedule into sprints; one test per sprint
The input/output space into classes; one representative per class

questions.explanation: Equivalence partitioning splits the input/output space into classes and tests one representative per class. The other options are unrelated. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: Boundary-value analysis tests what?

options.text: The edges of equivalence classes (e.g. B-1, B, B+1; empty/full/wrap)
The exact middle of each class only

questions.explanation: Boundary-value analysis tests the edges of equivalence classes. Middle-only, largest-only, and uniform-random miss where defects cluster. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Statement coverage is described as which kind of criterion?
questions.explanation: Statement coverage is the weakest criterion (a test can skip an if body). Path is strongest, and statement is not identical to path nor unrelated to testing. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: Path coverage is described as which kind of criterion?
questions.explanation: Path coverage is the strongest, usually infeasible criterion. It is not the weakest, not identical to statement coverage, and not a black-box technique. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: What does a fragile test do?

options.text: Breaks for reasons unrelated to a real defect

questions.explanation: A fragile test breaks without a real defect. It is not always-passing, production-only, or assertionless by definition. (Software Testing p.24)

questions.stem: What is 'Test under the UI'?

options.text: Testing the domain logic directly, bypassing the GUI
Testing only by clicking GUI widgets

questions.explanation: Test under the UI means testing domain logic directly, bypassing the GUI. Clicking widgets, pixel checks, and network testing are not it. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: What is fault injection?

options.text: Forcing dependency failures on demand (e.g. fail 1% of calls)

questions.explanation: Fault injection forces dependency failures on demand. It is not feature injection, automatic fault removal, or random input generation. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: What is TDD in one line?

options.text: Write the implementation before the test
Write the test before the implementation

questions.explanation: TDD means writing the test before the implementation. The other options describe traditional or no testing. (Software Testing p.35)

questions.stem: What is a defect test?

options.text: A test that proves no defects exist
A test of the GUI only
A test run only under load
A test that pins down a possible defect / over-written code

questions.explanation: A defect test pins down a possible defect or over-written code. It does not prove no defects exist, target only the GUI, or require load. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: What does the DRY principle stand for?
questions.explanation: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself, the refactoring goal of removing duplication. The other expansions are invented. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: A stub is best described as what?

options.text: A double that records and verifies calls
A double returning predefined data; lightest and most static
A real object with one method overridden

questions.explanation: A stub returns predefined data and is the lightest, most static double. Recording/verifying is a mock, real-with-override is a spy, and full in-memory is a fake. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: A mock is best described as what?

options.text: A double returning only predefined data
A real object that delegates by default
A double that records and verifies interactions; most powerful

questions.explanation: A mock records and verifies interactions and is the most powerful double. Canned-only is a stub, real-delegating is a spy, and simplified-working is a fake. (Software Testing p.59)

questions.stem: A spy is best described as what?

options.text: A double with no real behaviour
A partial mock wrapping a real object; delegates by default

questions.explanation: A spy is a partial mock wrapping a real object and delegates by default. No-real-behaviour is a mock, canned-only is a stub, and a coverage tool is unrelated. (Software Testing p.72-73)

questions.stem: What does verify() do in Mockito?

options.text: Asserts that a mock method was called

questions.explanation: verify() asserts a mock method was called. Stubbing is when().thenReturn(), creating is mock(), and injecting is @InjectMocks. (Software Testing p.69)

questions.stem: What does when().thenReturn() do in Mockito?

options.text: Stubs a return value for a condition

questions.explanation: when().thenReturn() stubs a return value. Verifying is verify(), suites use @RunWith(Suite.class), and disabling assertions is a JVM flag. (Software Testing p.69-70)

questions.stem: What does regression testing protect against?

options.text: New breakage of previously working functionality after a change

questions.explanation: Regression testing catches new breakage of previously working functionality. It is not about coverage, compilation speed, or mock counts. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which annotation marks a JUnit setup method that runs before each test?
questions.explanation: @Before runs before each test. @BeforeClass runs once, @Test marks a test, and @Mock creates a mock. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Which xUnit tool is named for mocking in the deck?
questions.explanation: Mockito is the mocking tool. DbUnit is for DB fixtures, CppUnit is C++ xUnit, and Sikuli does GUI image testing. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: According to TestLab1, an assertion should do what when it fails?
questions.explanation: TestLab1 says an assertion should stop the program, whereas an exception should let it continue. Continuing, logging-and-continuing, and retrying are not the assertion's role. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: What is the Mars Climate Orbiter the stock example of?

options.text: A specification/interface unit mismatch (m/s vs ft/s)

questions.explanation: Mars Orbiter is the stock example of a spec/interface unit mismatch. It is not a compiler bug, a GUI test, or a mocking example. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: A maintainer renames a public method during refactoring; many tests now fail to compile though behaviour is unchanged. Which sensitivity is this and what is the lesson?

options.text: Behavior sensitivity; the behaviour really changed
Data sensitivity; a fixture row changed
Interface sensitivity; test through stable interfaces and treat the breakage as non-defect noise

questions.explanation: Renaming an API couples tests to the interface shape, so this is interface sensitivity and the breakage is non-defect noise. Behaviour did not change (not behavior), no fixture changed (not data), and no environment changed (not context). (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: You have a stub returning a fixed clock time and you also call verify() on it to assert it was queried. What is the design smell?

options.text: You are over-specifying: verifying a pure query turns a lightweight stub into a behaviour-coupled mock, increasing fragility
You should make the clock final to ease mocking
You must run the test under load
You should disable assertions for clock tests

questions.explanation: Verifying a pure query (the clock read) over-specifies the test, coupling it to interaction and making it fragile; a stub should answer without verification. Making the clock final would block Mockito, load is irrelevant, and disabling assertions is unrelated. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: Why can a unit test suite pass on both modules yet the integrated system still fail, per the Mars Orbiter lesson?

options.text: Because unit tests always have wrong oracles
Each module is internally consistent, so the defect lives in the shared specification/interface, not in either SUT
Because assertions are off by default

questions.explanation: Each module passes because it is internally consistent; the fault is in the shared spec/interface, invisible to either unit test. Oracles are not always wrong, integration testing is possible, and assertion defaults are not the cause. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: Order these by increasing test-double power/coupling: stub, spy, mock. Which ordering and rationale is correct?

options.text: Mock < stub < spy, because mocks are simplest
Spy < mock < stub, because stubs verify the most
Stub (canned data, no verification) < spy (real object, override/verify a few) < mock (records and verifies everything)
They are all equal in power

questions.explanation: The stub is lightest (canned data, no verification), the spy is a real-object hybrid, and the mock is the most powerful (records and verifies). The other orderings contradict the deck's definitions. (Software Testing p.59-60, p.72)

questions.stem: A test asserts an exact log message string emitted during an operation, and it breaks when the message wording is tweaked. Which sensitivity, and what TDD principle was violated?

options.text: Interface sensitivity; the method was renamed
Data sensitivity; a database row changed
Behavior sensitivity from over-specifying incidental behaviour; the test should assert only the essential outcome

questions.explanation: Asserting an incidental log string over-specifies behaviour, so a harmless wording tweak breaks the test; tests should assert essential outcomes. No rename (interface), no fixture (data), and no environment (context) changed. (Software Testing p.26)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say 100% branch coverage on the queue still does not prove the queue is correct?

options.text: Branch coverage exercises both outcomes of each decision but not every input/value combination or missing-logic case, so bugs can persist (p.6)
Because branch coverage is weaker than statement coverage
Because the queue has no branches
Because coverage tools cannot measure branches

questions.explanation: Branch coverage takes both decision outcomes but not all input combinations or missing logic, so Dijkstra's limit still applies. Branch is stronger than statement, the queue does branch, and tools can measure branches. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: In the TDD Borrow-Book flow, why must the alternative-scenario test be written as a NEW failing test rather than just editing the implementation?

options.text: Because editing implementations is illegal in Java
TDD forbids writing production code without a failing test that demands it, so the new code (the 10-book guard) requires a new red test first
Because the main-scenario test already covers the guard
Because refactoring must happen before any new behaviour

questions.explanation: TDD says you cannot add code without a failing test demanding it, so the 10-book guard needs a new red test first. Editing is legal, the main test does not cover the guard, and refactoring is the third step, not a prerequisite for new behaviour. (Software Testing p.39, p.45)

questions.stem: A team disables Java assertions in production for speed, but a checkRep had a side effect inside it. What is the consequence and which rule was broken?

options.text: Nothing changes, since assertions never affect behaviour
The program fails to compile, violating Rule 1
Disabling assertions now changes program behaviour because the side effect no longer runs, violating Rule 2 (no side effects)
Coverage drops to zero, violating Rule 3

questions.explanation: A side effect inside an assertion means disabling assertions changes behaviour, violating Rule 2. Assertions with side effects DO affect behaviour, compilation is unaffected, and coverage is not Rule 3's concern. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: You must test that a method throws IllegalArgumentException on bad input and also inspect the exception's message. Which idiom fits best?

options.text: try { m(); fail(); } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { /* assert e's message */ }

questions.explanation: When you must inspect the exception's contents, use try/fail/catch. The annotation form cannot inspect the message, the assertTrue idiom does not test throwing, and verify is for mocks. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: You only need to confirm a method throws a specific exception type, nothing more. Which idiom is most concise?
questions.explanation: When only the type matters, the annotation @Test(expected=...) is most concise. try/fail/catch is for inspecting contents, assertNull does not test throwing, and when().thenReturn stubs a mock. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: A unit test for borrowBook would otherwise call a real OverdueService over the network. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Let the test hit the real network for realism
Replace OverdueService with a Mockito mock/stub so the test stays a single isolated code-path
Delete the OverdueService dependency from production
Run the test only when the network is up

questions.explanation: Replace the external service with a mock/stub to keep the unit test isolated and deterministic. Hitting the real network, deleting production code, and network-conditional runs all defeat unit-test isolation. (Software Testing p.58, TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Which scenario is the right place to use a spy rather than a mock?

options.text: You need a completely fake object with all canned responses
You need only a canned value for a single query
You want mostly-real behaviour of an object but need to override or verify one or two methods
You need to inject dependencies by constructor

questions.explanation: A spy fits when you want mostly-real behaviour with a couple of overridden/verified methods. A fully fake object is a mock, a single canned query is a stub, and constructor injection is @InjectMocks. (Software Testing p.72-73)

questions.stem: Given the sqrt spec, which input belongs to its OWN equivalence class for float testing because it is a special edge value?
questions.explanation: Float special values like NaN, -0.0, and overflow form their own equivalence classes. Ordinary integers like 5, 2, or 100 fall in the normal positive class. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: A maintainer wants to refactor a tangled JHotDraw method but has no tests. According to the TDD/change-process linkage, what should happen first?

options.text: Write a covering test suite (the safety net) before refactoring, since refactoring requires a good test suite
Refactor immediately; tests can come later
Skip refactoring entirely and ship the tangle

questions.explanation: Refactoring requires a good test suite as a safety net, so write covering tests first. Refactoring without tests, disabling assertions, or skipping refactoring all forfeit safety. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say the oracle choice is 'half the art of testing' even when the SUT is correct?

options.text: The oracle compiles the SUT, so it must be fast
A wrong or expensive oracle can fail a correct SUT or make the test impractical, so the pass/fail judgement quality bounds the test's value
The oracle generates inputs, so it must be random
The oracle measures coverage, so it must be exhaustive

questions.explanation: The oracle is the pass/fail judge, so a wrong/expensive oracle undermines even a correct SUT. The oracle does not compile, generate inputs, or measure coverage. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: A CI pipeline re-runs the full automated suite on every commit. Which lecture concept does this most directly implement?

options.text: Regression testing as the heartbeat of Verification/CI

questions.explanation: Re-running the suite on every commit is regression testing, the heartbeat of Verification/CI. Partitioning, boundary analysis, and fault injection are test-design techniques, not the CI re-run loop. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which statement about manual versus automated tests is correct per the deck?

options.text: Manual tests are cheaper and run on every change
Both are equally cheap to execute
Manual tests are expensive and can't be run often; automated tests run on every change

questions.explanation: Manual tests are expensive and infrequent; automated tests run on every change and enable regression. The distractors invert cost or deny regression capability. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which combination of techniques does the worked-example pipeline exercise end-to-end?

options.text: Incompleteness-aware design, levels, TDD cycle, JUnit, mocks, assertions, and regression

questions.explanation: The worked example exercises incompleteness-awareness, levels, the TDD cycle, JUnit, mocks, assertions, and regression. The narrow options omit most of the pipeline. (Software Testing p.5-81)

questions.stem: What does the guide say is the 'evidence you present at the Verification step' for a JHotDraw change?

options.text: The body of JUnit unit tests covering the feature's domain logic, with boundary cases, mocks/stubs, and assertions
A list of refactored class names

questions.explanation: The Verification evidence is the documented body of JUnit tests (domain logic, boundaries, mocks/stubs, assertions). A screenshot, a class list, and a benchmark are not the evidence. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call assertions a bridge to Prefactoring and Postfactoring specifically?

options.text: Because assertions replace the change process
Self-checking, invariant-documenting code stays testable, and a passing assertion-backed suite makes cleaning code before/after a change safe
Because Postfactoring runs only manual tests

questions.explanation: Assertions keep code self-checking and testable, so an assertion-backed suite makes Prefactoring/Postfactoring safe. They do not replace the process, get deleted by Prefactoring, or imply manual tests. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Which is the correct contrast between an assertion and an exception for handling a user typing an invalid password?

options.text: Invalid password should trigger an assertion that stops the program
Both an assertion and an exception are equally appropriate
Neither should be used; ignore the input
Invalid password is expected input and gets an exception; an assertion would be wrong here

questions.explanation: An invalid password is an expected runtime error and gets an exception, not an assertion (which is for 'should never happen'). The other options misuse assertions or ignore the input. (Software Testing p.18, TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: After Concept Location and Impact Analysis identify the changed modules, what does Lecture 7 say you must do with their tests?

options.text: (Re)write and run the unit tests for exactly those impacted modules as your Verification evidence
Run only the GUI record-and-play scripts
Test every module in the codebase manually
Skip testing because impact analysis already verified the change

questions.explanation: You (re)write and run unit tests for the impacted modules as Verification evidence. GUI-only scripts, manual whole-codebase testing, and skipping testing are all wrong. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which JUnit assertion would you use to confirm a returned object reference is not null?
questions.explanation: assertNotNull(obj) confirms a non-null reference. assertNull asserts the opposite, assertTrue needs a boolean, and fail() always fails. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: What is the goal of testing, as the deck's title slide phrases it?

options.text: To make software fail (on purpose, so defects surface in the lab)

questions.explanation: The deck's title is 'Software Testing: How to make software fail!' so defects surface in the lab not production. It is not about speed, proving bug-freedom, or documenting the UI. (Software Testing p.1)

questions.stem: Integration testing focuses on what?

options.text: Components working together across their interfaces

questions.explanation: Integration testing checks components working together across interfaces. Single-method isolation is unit testing, whole-system is system testing, and random inputs is random testing. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Differential testing uses what as its oracle?
questions.explanation: Differential testing uses a reference implementation as the oracle. A constant, a coverage report, and a random seed are not oracles. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: What is an assertion's purpose in code?

options.text: A way to handle invalid user input
An executable check for an invariant that must always be true

questions.explanation: An assertion is an executable invariant check. Handling user input is an exception's job, and assertions are neither loggers nor test runners. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: How many rules of assertions does the deck give?

options.text: Three (not for error handling, no side effects, no silly assertions)

questions.explanation: The deck gives three rules: not for error handling, no side effects, no silly assertions. The other counts are wrong. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: What does @AfterClass do in JUnit 4?

options.text: Runs once, statically, after all tests in the class

questions.explanation: @AfterClass runs once, statically, after all tests. After-each is @After, before-each is @Before, and marking a test is @Test. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Which JUnit assertion checks two values are equal?
questions.explanation: assertEquals(expected, actual) checks equality. The other names are not the deck's listed equality assertion. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: Mockito is used in conjunction with which framework?
questions.explanation: Mockito is used in conjunction with JUnit. It is not tied to Spring Boot only, nor is it CppUnit or Sikuli. (Software Testing p.58)

questions.stem: What does @Mock require to be initialized?
questions.explanation: @Mock fields require MockitoAnnotations.initMocks(this) or they stay null. They are not new-ed, do not need @BeforeClass specifically, and do not self-initialize. (Software Testing p.65)

questions.stem: Acceptance tests are based on what?

options.text: The requirements, defined with the user

questions.explanation: Acceptance tests are based on the requirements and defined with the user. Compiler output, fuzzing, and CI config are not their basis. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about manual tests' frequency?

options.text: They can't be run often because they are expensive
They run automatically on every commit
They are cheaper than automated tests

questions.explanation: Manual tests can't be run often because they are expensive. They do not auto-run on commits, are not cheaper, and do not give immediate regression feedback. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: In the tester's-mindset slide, what does the developer want?
questions.explanation: The developer wants the code to succeed; the tester wants it to fail. Coverage and test counts are not the framing. (Software Testing p.83)

questions.stem: Which is NOT something Mockito can mock?
questions.explanation: Mockito cannot mock final classes (also anonymous classes and primitive types). Interfaces, abstract classes, and concrete non-final classes are mockable. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: What is the boundary-value rule of thumb for a boundary B?
questions.explanation: The rule of thumb is B-1, B, B+1. The other options skip part of the cluster where defects live. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: A test reads a row with CPR '1234651234' from a shared live database that another test mutates, causing intermittent failures. Which sensitivity and which cure apply?

options.text: Data sensitivity; own and control your test data (define your own Testdata) instead of sharing mutable live state
Interface sensitivity; stabilize the method signatures
Behavior sensitivity; accept the changed behaviour

questions.explanation: Depending on shared mutable data is data sensitivity; the cure is owning/controlling your test data (the lab's Testdata). Signature stabilization (interface), clock injection (context), and accepting change (behavior) do not fix shared-state coupling. (Software Testing p.27, TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Why does the guide treat path coverage's infeasibility as 'the practical face of the p.5 impossibility result'?

options.text: Because paths cannot be defined for any program
Loops make the number of paths combinatorially explode, mirroring the theoretical impossibility of fully covering an infinite space
Because branch coverage subsumes path coverage
Because statement coverage is the strongest criterion

questions.explanation: Loops cause a combinatorial path explosion, echoing the p.5 impossibility of complete coverage; you approximate with representative paths. Paths are definable, branch does not subsume path, and statement coverage is the weakest. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: A team achieves 100% statement coverage but a defect remains in an untaken else-branch. Which coverage criterion would have forced a test there, and why is even that not proof?

options.text: Path coverage is weaker, so it would not help
Statement coverage already covers all branches
Branch coverage would force the else; but per Dijkstra it still tests structure, not all inputs/values, so correctness is unproven
No criterion can ever reach an else-branch

questions.explanation: Branch coverage forces both decision outcomes, including the else, but even it tests structure not all inputs, so correctness is unproven (p.6). Path is stronger not weaker, statement does not cover all branches, and else-branches are reachable. (Software Testing p.11, p.6)
